title: Religion, the Churches, and Positive Christianity
summary: The Nazi regime's relationship with Christianity was one of calculated ambiguity, tactical concession, and progressive hostility — driven by ideological incompatibility between Christian universalism and Nazi racial doctrine.

sections.heading: Positive Christianity: The Programme Clause
sections.text: Point 24 of the 1920 NSDAP programme called for 'positive Christianity' — a deliberately undefined term intended to secure the support of Germany's overwhelmingly Christian population while committing the party to nothing doctrinally specific. In Nazi usage, 'positive Christianity' generally meant Christianity purged of its Jewish origins (the Old Testament was particularly targeted), reinterpreted as a Germanic faith emphasising heroism, sacrifice, and racial solidarity rather than humility, sin, and universalism. It was never given consistent institutional form. Hitler personally expressed private contempt for Christianity in his Table Talk and other recorded conversations, describing it as suited only to 'weak people'.

sections.heading: The Reich Concordat with the Vatican, July 1933
sections.text: On 20 July 1933, Hitler's government signed a Reichskonkordat with the Holy See — the most important international agreement the new regime concluded in its first year. The Concordat guaranteed the freedom of Catholic institutions and the rights of the Catholic Church in Germany; in return, the Vatican agreed that Catholic clergy would refrain from political activity, and dissolved the Catholic Centre Party (which had already dissolved itself under pressure). Pope Pius XI had serious reservations but accepted the agreement as the best available protection for German Catholics. Hitler regarded the Concordat as a major diplomatic success and an implicit international legitimation of his government. The Concordat's provisions were immediately and systematically violated by the regime.

sections.heading: The German Christians and the Reich Church
sections.text: Within German Protestantism, the 'German Christians' (Deutsche Christen) were a pro-Nazi faction that sought to 'synchronise' the Protestant churches with National Socialist ideology. They won elections in Protestant church councils in July 1933 and supported the appointment of Ludwig Müller as Reich Bishop. The German Christians sought to remove the Old Testament from Christian scripture, deny Jesus's Jewish heritage, and apply the Aryan paragraph (excluding Jewish converts) to church offices. Their theology was rejected by a significant part of German Protestantism, leading to the formation of the opposing Confessing Church (Bekennende Kirche) in 1934.

sections.heading: The Confessing Church and Resistance
sections.text: The Confessing Church (Bekennende Kirche), formed at the Barmen Synod of May 1934, rejected the German Christians' theological innovations and the subordination of the church to the state. Its founding declaration — the Barmen Declaration, drafted principally by theologian Karl Barth — insisted that the church was bound only to Christ, not to any political authority. Key figures included Martin Niemöller (U-boat commander turned pastor, arrested in 1937 and sent to Dachau), Dietrich Bonhoeffer (arrested in 1943 for his role in the military resistance, executed April 1945), and Bishop Theophil Wurm of Württemberg. The Confessing Church was not a resistance movement in the political sense — it objected primarily to state control of the church rather than Nazi ideology broadly — but it represented the most organised institutional opposition to regime encroachments.

sections.heading: The Mit Brennender Sorge Encyclical, March 1937
sections.text: On 21 March 1937, Pope Pius XI issued the encyclical Mit Brennender Sorge ('With Burning Anxiety'), written in German and secretly distributed to German Catholic churches for simultaneous reading from pulpits on Palm Sunday. It condemned the violation of the 1933 Concordat, the deification of race and nation, and the substitution of a 'national god' for the God of Christian faith — though it did not explicitly name Hitler or the NSDAP. The Gestapo worked frantically to confiscate copies; the Sunday reading nonetheless reached millions of German Catholics. The regime's response was escalated harassment of the Catholic Church, including criminal prosecutions of priests on fabricated currency and morality charges.

sections.heading: The SS and Neo-Pagan Ideology
sections.text: The SS under Heinrich Himmler developed the most systematic attempt to replace Christianity with a Germanic neo-pagan worldview. Himmler was fascinated by Nordic mythology, runic symbolism, and the supposed spiritual heritage of the ancient Germanic peoples. The SS developed its own lifecycle ceremonies (birth, marriage, death) to replace church sacraments, and its own calendar of 'Germanic' seasonal celebrations to replace Christian festivals. The Externsteine rock formation in Teutoburg Forest was identified as a sacred Germanic site and subjected to pseudo-archaeological investigation. Himmler's SS castle at Wewelsburg in Westphalia was designed as the symbolic centre of an SS order with quasi-religious pretensions. These projects remained largely on the fringes of the regime; the German population remained overwhelmingly Christian in practice.

sections.heading: Euthanasia and Church Protest
sections.text: The T4 euthanasia programme — the systematic murder of disabled Germans deemed 'lives unworthy of life' — generated the most successful public protest the churches mounted against any Nazi programme. Beginning in 1939 and officially suspended in August 1941, T4 killed approximately 70,000 people in six killing centres using carbon monoxide gas. Bishop Clemens von Galen of Münster delivered three sermons in August 1941 openly denouncing the programme, calling it murder. Copies circulated throughout Germany and were dropped by RAF aircraft over the Reich. The Gestapo considered arresting von Galen but feared it would alienate the Catholic population of Westphalia; Hitler was reportedly furious but deferred action. T4 was officially suspended — though it continued covertly — partly in response to the protest.

sources.ref: Burleigh, Michael. The Third Reich: A New History. Hill and Wang, 2000. Chapter 5.
Matheson, Peter. The Third Reich and the Christian Churches. T&T Clark, 1981.
Kershaw, Ian. The Nazi Dictatorship: Problems and Perspectives of Interpretation (4th ed.). Arnold, 2000.
Scholder, Klaus. The Churches and the Third Reich (2 vols). Fortress Press, 1987–88.
Steigmann-Gall, Richard. The Holy Reich: Nazi Conceptions of Christianity. Cambridge University Press, 2003.
